title: Infective Endocarditis
oneLiner: Life-threatening infection of heart valves (native/prosthetic) or endocardium; treat as sepsis with embolic risk — suspect in fever + murmur + risk factors.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Septic shock / acute valve failure:** Severe hypotension, refractory hypoxia, acute pulmonary oedema, or new loud murmur + haemodynamic instability. Finding: **NEWS2 ≥ 7 + new regurgitant murmur** → immediate senior + echo.
- **Cerebral embolic stroke / mycotic aneurysm rupture:** Acute focal neurology, sudden severe headache, or decreased GCS. Finding: **Acute neurological deficit + fever** → CT head stat (rule out bleed) before antibiotics.
- **Cardiac tamponade (if prosthetic IE):** Muffled heart sounds, elevated JVP, pulsus paradoxus, hypotension. Finding: **Beck's triad + fever** → urgent echo.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fever (often high, swinging, rigors) — most common
  - Night sweats, malaise, weight loss
  - Dyspnoea, chest pain, palpitations (if valve dysfunction)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **New or changing cardiac murmur** (most specific)
  - Signs of heart failure (pulmonary oedema, raised JVP)
  - Peripheral stigmata: splinter haemorrhages, Janeway lesions (painless palmar), Osler's nodes (tender finger pulps), Roth spots (retinal), petechiae (conjunctival/palate)
  - Splenomegaly (subacute IE)
  - Neurological signs (embolic stroke, meningitis)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Sepsis from other source (pneumonia, UTI, line infection):** No new murmur, no peripheral stigmata, source elsewhere.
- **Acute rheumatic fever:** Migratory arthritis, chorea, erythema marginatum; history of sore throat; anti-streptolysin O titres.
- **Intracranial haemorrhage (if neurological presentation):** No fever, CT head shows bleed; consider mycotic aneurysm if fever + sudden SAH.
- **Non-infective endocarditis (marantic/Libman-Sacks):** No fever, often SLE/APS, negative blood cultures.
- **Atrial myxoma:** Tumour plop, positional syncope, no fever; echo shows mobile mass on left atrium.
- **Splitter:** "Fever + new murmur" is endocarditis until proven otherwise. If fever absent and murmur present → think non-infective causes.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG: look for heart block (perivalvular abscess) — new PR prolongation/Mobitz II is a red flag.
  - Urinalysis: haematuria (embolic glomerulonephritis)
  - Blood culture sets × 2 (from separate sites) **before antibiotics**
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia, leucocytosis), CRP, ESR (both raised)
  - U&Es, LFTs (pre-renal failure, hepatic congestion)
  - Blood cultures — positive in 85% (if negative, think HACEK, Q fever, or prior antibiotics)
  - ± serology for Coxiella burnetii, Bartonella, Brucella (culture-negative IE)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Transthoracic echo (TTE)** — first-line; sensitivity ~70%, better for native valves
  - **Transoesophageal echo (TOE)** — if TTE negative/high clinical suspicion, prosthetic valve, or abscess; sensitivity >90%
  - Chest X-ray: heart size, pulmonary oedema, septic emboli
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **Modified Duke Criteria** — major (positive blood culture, echo evidence of vegetation/abscess) and minor (fever, predisposing condition, vascular phenomena, immunological phenomena). Definite IE: 2 major or 1 major + 3 minor or 5 minor.
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood cultures × 2 before antibiotics** — omission is unforgivable; also **TOE** if prosthetic valve or high suspicion with negative TTE.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Sepsis 6: oxygen, cultures, IV antibiotics (after cultures), IV fluids, lactate, monitor urine output.
  - If haemodynamically unstable or acute valve failure — **call cardiology / cardiac surgery urgently** for possible valve replacement.
  - Start empiric antibiotics **only after cultures**, but do not delay if septic: use local endocarditis protocol.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV antibiotics per microbiology guidance (usually 4–6 weeks).
  - Daily review for heart failure, embolic events, and antibiotic toxicity.
  - Monitor renal function (gentamicin/vancomycin levels).
  - Repeat blood cultures at 48h-72h to confirm clearance.
  - Arrange outpatient parenteral antibiotic therapy (OPAT) if stable.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete course (typically 4–6 weeks IV, longer for prosthetic or staphylococcal IE).
  - Dental review and prophylaxis as per NICE (no routine prophylaxis for dental procedures in UK unless high risk per specialist).
  - Valve surgery elective: if large vegetation (>10 mm), recurrent emboli, heart failure, perivalvular abscess, fungal IE.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline at 1L/8h unless renal impairment or heart failure.
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless high bleeding risk (cerebral emboli, mycotic aneurysm).
  - NBM if planned for surgery or TOE.
  - Observations: 4-hourly (NEWS2) minimum; if septic, hourly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (empiric — check local microbiologist):**
  - Native valve, community-acquired: **Benzylpenicillin** 1.2–2.4 g IV 4-hourly + **Gentamicin** 1 mg/kg IV 12-hourly (adjusted for renal function) ± **Flucloxacillin** 2 g IV 4-hourly (if staph suspected).
  - Prosthetic valve / healthcare-associated: **Vancomycin** 15–20 mg/kg IV 12-hourly (target trough 10–15 mg/L) + **Gentamicin** + **Rifampicin** 300–600 mg PO/IV 12-hourly.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Penicillin allergy: Vancomycin ± Gentamicin.
  - HACEK organisms (Haemophilus, Actinobacillus, Cardiobacterium, Eikenella, Kingella): Ceftriaxone 2 g IV 24-hourly.
  - Culture-negative: Doxycycline + Gentamicin (if Q fever suspected) — discuss with ID.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Gentamicin – nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity; avoid if myasthenia gravis.
  - Vancomycin – red man syndrome (infuse over ≥1h); adjust dose per renal function.
  - Rifampicin – potent CYP450 inducer; check drug interactions (warfarin, OCP, antiretrovirals).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Gentamicin: use Hartford dosing; check levels pre-dose after third dose (trough <1 mg/L for once-daily; for twice-daily, trough <2 mg/L).
  - Vancomycin: adjust based on eGFR; trough after 4th dose (target 10–15 mg/L, 15–20 mg/L for complicated).
  - Rifampicin: no renal adjustment; caution in liver disease.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Gentamicin – not checking levels before second dose** leads to nephrotoxicity; **also giving a stat dose of gentamicin without checking renal function** in elderly.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Embolic stroke, septic emboli to lung/spleen/kidney/brain
  - Acute heart failure (valve destruction)
  - Perivalvular abscess → heart block, purulent pericarditis/tamponade
  - Mycotic aneurysm (intracranial or visceral) → rupture
  - Acute renal failure (immune complex glomerulonephritis or sepsis)
- **Chronic:**
  - Progressive valve regurgitation/stenosis → chronic heart failure
  - Chronic kidney disease
  - Residual neurological deficits
  - Relapse (if incomplete treatment or resistant organism)
sections.Prognosis: - Overall mortality ~20–30% (higher for staphylococcal, prosthetic, or complicated IE). With early surgery and appropriate antibiotics, survival improves. Long-term risk of recurrence (~5–10% in first year). Valve damage often persists.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always obtain 2 sets of blood cultures from different sites before any antibiotics** — even if patient is in septic shock, a 30-minute delay for cultures is acceptable.
- **Remember TOE if prosthetic valve or TTE negative with high suspicion** — it changes management in >20% of cases.
- **Involve the Endocarditis Team (cardiologist, microbiologist, cardiac surgeon) early** — improves outcomes.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT wait for echo results to start antibiotics in unstable sepsis** — draw cultures, then start empiric antibiotics.
- **Do NOT give gentamicin without checking baseline renal function and arranging level monitoring** — a single toxic dose can cause permanent renal injury.
- **Do NOT discharge a patient on OPAT without arranging follow-up echo** — need to confirm vegetation resolution and assess valve function.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** New focal weakness, severe headache, chest pain, shortness of breath, rigors, or haematuria.
- **Follow-up:** Outpatient cardiology + microbiology in 2 weeks; repeat echo at 3–6 months; dental assessment.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (Reg/Consultant) if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 6 or lactate > 4 mmol/L
  - New heart block on ECG (PR prolongation) or new murmur
  - Acute neurological deterioration (suspected embolus/bleed)
  - Pyrexia persisting >48–72h on appropriate antibiotics
- **Consider ICU if:**
  - Septic shock requiring vasopressors
  - Mechanical ventilation for pulmonary oedema
  - Intracranial haemorrhage / post-surgery
- **Crash team:**
  - Cardiac arrest; bradycardia with haemodynamic instability (new heart block)

rawMarkdown:
## Infective Endocarditis
**One-liner:** Life-threatening infection of heart valves (native/prosthetic) or endocardium; treat as sepsis with embolic risk — suspect in fever + murmur + risk factors.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Septic shock / acute valve failure:** Severe hypotension, refractory hypoxia, acute pulmonary oedema, or new loud murmur + haemodynamic instability. Finding: **NEWS2 ≥ 7 + new regurgitant murmur** → immediate senior + echo.
- **Cerebral embolic stroke / mycotic aneurysm rupture:** Acute focal neurology, sudden severe headache, or decreased GCS. Finding: **Acute neurological deficit + fever** → CT head stat (rule out bleed) before antibiotics.
- **Cardiac tamponade (if prosthetic IE):** Muffled heart sounds, elevated JVP, pulsus paradoxus, hypotension. Finding: **Beck's triad + fever** → urgent echo.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fever (often high, swinging, rigors) — most common
  - Night sweats, malaise, weight loss
  - Dyspnoea, chest pain, palpitations (if valve dysfunction)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **New or changing cardiac murmur** (most specific)
  - Signs of heart failure (pulmonary oedema, raised JVP)
  - Peripheral stigmata: splinter haemorrhages, Janeway lesions (painless palmar), Osler's nodes (tender finger pulps), Roth spots (retinal), petechiae (conjunctival/palate)
  - Splenomegaly (subacute IE)
  - Neurological signs (embolic stroke, meningitis)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Sepsis from other source (pneumonia, UTI, line infection):** No new murmur, no peripheral stigmata, source elsewhere.
- **Acute rheumatic fever:** Migratory arthritis, chorea, erythema marginatum; history of sore throat; anti-streptolysin O titres.
- **Intracranial haemorrhage (if neurological presentation):** No fever, CT head shows bleed; consider mycotic aneurysm if fever + sudden SAH.
- **Non-infective endocarditis (marantic/Libman-Sacks):** No fever, often SLE/APS, negative blood cultures.
- **Atrial myxoma:** Tumour plop, positional syncope, no fever; echo shows mobile mass on left atrium.
- **Splitter:** "Fever + new murmur" is endocarditis until proven otherwise. If fever absent and murmur present → think non-infective causes.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG: look for heart block (perivalvular abscess) — new PR prolongation/Mobitz II is a red flag.
  - Urinalysis: haematuria (embolic glomerulonephritis)
  - Blood culture sets × 2 (from separate sites) **before antibiotics**
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia, leucocytosis), CRP, ESR (both raised)
  - U&Es, LFTs (pre-renal failure, hepatic congestion)
  - Blood cultures — positive in 85% (if negative, think HACEK, Q fever, or prior antibiotics)
  - ± serology for Coxiella burnetii, Bartonella, Brucella (culture-negative IE)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Transthoracic echo (TTE)** — first-line; sensitivity ~70%, better for native valves
  - **Transoesophageal echo (TOE)** — if TTE negative/high clinical suspicion, prosthetic valve, or abscess; sensitivity >90%
  - Chest X-ray: heart size, pulmonary oedema, septic emboli
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **Modified Duke Criteria** — major (positive blood culture, echo evidence of vegetation/abscess) and minor (fever, predisposing condition, vascular phenomena, immunological phenomena). Definite IE: 2 major or 1 major + 3 minor or 5 minor.
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood cultures × 2 before antibiotics** — omission is unforgivable; also **TOE** if prosthetic valve or high suspicion with negative TTE.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Sepsis 6: oxygen, cultures, IV antibiotics (after cultures), IV fluids, lactate, monitor urine output.
  - If haemodynamically unstable or acute valve failure — **call cardiology / cardiac surgery urgently** for possible valve replacement.
  - Start empiric antibiotics **only after cultures**, but do not delay if septic: use local endocarditis protocol.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV antibiotics per microbiology guidance (usually 4–6 weeks).
  - Daily review for heart failure, embolic events, and antibiotic toxicity.
  - Monitor renal function (gentamicin/vancomycin levels).
  - Repeat blood cultures at 48h-72h to confirm clearance.
  - Arrange outpatient parenteral antibiotic therapy (OPAT) if stable.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete course (typically 4–6 weeks IV, longer for prosthetic or staphylococcal IE).
  - Dental review and prophylaxis as per NICE (no routine prophylaxis for dental procedures in UK unless high risk per specialist).
  - Valve surgery elective: if large vegetation (>10 mm), recurrent emboli, heart failure, perivalvular abscess, fungal IE.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline at 1L/8h unless renal impairment or heart failure.
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless high bleeding risk (cerebral emboli, mycotic aneurysm).
  - NBM if planned for surgery or TOE.
  - Observations: 4-hourly (NEWS2) minimum; if septic, hourly.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (empiric — check local microbiologist):**
  - Native valve, community-acquired: **Benzylpenicillin** 1.2–2.4 g IV 4-hourly + **Gentamicin** 1 mg/kg IV 12-hourly (adjusted for renal function) ± **Flucloxacillin** 2 g IV 4-hourly (if staph suspected).
  - Prosthetic valve / healthcare-associated: **Vancomycin** 15–20 mg/kg IV 12-hourly (target trough 10–15 mg/L) + **Gentamicin** + **Rifampicin** 300–600 mg PO/IV 12-hourly.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Penicillin allergy: Vancomycin ± Gentamicin.
  - HACEK organisms (Haemophilus, Actinobacillus, Cardiobacterium, Eikenella, Kingella): Ceftriaxone 2 g IV 24-hourly.
  - Culture-negative: Doxycycline + Gentamicin (if Q fever suspected) — discuss with ID.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Gentamicin – nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity; avoid if myasthenia gravis.
  - Vancomycin – red man syndrome (infuse over ≥1h); adjust dose per renal function.
  - Rifampicin – potent CYP450 inducer; check drug interactions (warfarin, OCP, antiretrovirals).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Gentamicin: use Hartford dosing; check levels pre-dose after third dose (trough <1 mg/L for once-daily; for twice-daily, trough <2 mg/L).
  - Vancomycin: adjust based on eGFR; trough after 4th dose (target 10–15 mg/L, 15–20 mg/L for complicated).
  - Rifampicin: no renal adjustment; caution in liver disease.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Gentamicin – not checking levels before second dose** leads to nephrotoxicity; **also giving a stat dose of gentamicin without checking renal function** in elderly.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Embolic stroke, septic emboli to lung/spleen/kidney/brain
  - Acute heart failure (valve destruction)
  - Perivalvular abscess → heart block, purulent pericarditis/tamponade
  - Mycotic aneurysm (intracranial or visceral) → rupture
  - Acute renal failure (immune complex glomerulonephritis or sepsis)
- **Chronic:**
  - Progressive valve regurgitation/stenosis → chronic heart failure
  - Chronic kidney disease
  - Residual neurological deficits
  - Relapse (if incomplete treatment or resistant organism)

### Prognosis
- Overall mortality ~20–30% (higher for staphylococcal, prosthetic, or complicated IE). With early surgery and appropriate antibiotics, survival improves. Long-term risk of recurrence (~5–10% in first year). Valve damage often persists.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always obtain 2 sets of blood cultures from different sites before any antibiotics** — even if patient is in septic shock, a 30-minute delay for cultures is acceptable.
- **Remember TOE if prosthetic valve or TTE negative with high suspicion** — it changes management in >20% of cases.
- **Involve the Endocarditis Team (cardiologist, microbiologist, cardiac surgeon) early** — improves outcomes.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT wait for echo results to start antibiotics in unstable sepsis** — draw cultures, then start empiric antibiotics.
- **Do NOT give gentamicin without checking baseline renal function and arranging level monitoring** — a single toxic dose can cause permanent renal injury.
- **Do NOT discharge a patient on OPAT without arranging follow-up echo** — need to confirm vegetation resolution and assess valve function.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** New focal weakness, severe headache, chest pain, shortness of breath, rigors, or haematuria.
- **Follow-up:** Outpatient cardiology + microbiology in 2 weeks; repeat echo at 3–6 months; dental assessment.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (Reg/Consultant) if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 6 or lactate > 4 mmol/L
  - New heart block on ECG (PR prolongation) or new murmur
  - Acute neurological deterioration (suspected embolus/bleed)
  - Pyrexia persisting >48–72h on appropriate antibiotics
- **Consider ICU if:**
  - Septic shock requiring vasopressors
  - Mechanical ventilation for pulmonary oedema
  - Intracranial haemorrhage / post-surgery
- **Crash team:**
  - Cardiac arrest; bradycardia with haemodynamic instability (new heart block)
